perturbation.original_text: iv) "Restricted Period" means during the Shareholder's employment with the Company or any of the Company's Affiliates and continuing for a period of three (3) years following the date of termination, but in all events for a period of not less than five (5) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: iv) "Restricted Period" means during the Shareholder's employment with the Company or any of the Company's Affiliates and continuing for a period of ten (10) years following the date of termination, but in all events for a period of not less than fifteen (15) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text extends the non-compete period to 10 years post-termination and sets an absolute minimum of 15 years from the agreement date. This may be deemed unreasonably long and unenforceable under Ohio law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time and scope. A 10-year post-termination restriction, with a 15-year minimum overall, may be considered unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, especially depending on the nature of the employment and the scope of the restriction. While Ohio courts assess reasonableness on a case-by-case basis, this extended duration increases the likelihood of it being deemed overly broad.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the restricted period, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Ohio law. The original text had a period of three years post-termination with a minimum of five years from the agreement date, while the changed text increased this to ten years post-termination with a minimum of fifteen years from the agreement date, which drastically changes the restrictions on the employee. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address non-compete enforceability, it highlights Ohio's regulatory interest in contracts that restrict trade. The LLM explanation indicates the changes could increase the likelihood of the agreement being deemed overly broad based on Ohio law.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder agrees that each of the covenants set forth in this Section 1 and in Section 2 is a separate and distinct covenant, independent of others and any other provision of this Agreement, and that the illegality or invalidity of any one, or more of the covenants or any part of one or more of them, will not render the others illegal or invalid.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder agrees that if any of the covenants set forth in this Section 1 and in Section 2 is deemed illegal or invalid for any reason, then all other covenants in this agreement shall also be deemed illegal and invalid.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a severability clause. The modified text makes the entire agreement invalid if any clause is deemed illegal. This contradicts standard severability principles and applicable Ohio law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1.47 codifies the principle of severability, stating that if any provision of a statute or document is found invalid, the remaining provisions should remain in effect if they are capable of being executed in accordance with the expressed intention of the parties. The modified text directly contradicts this principle by causing the entire agreement to fail if any part is invalid, regardless of the possibility of severing the invalid provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1.47 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1.47 | Presumptions in enactment of statutes. Ohio Revised Code / General Provisions / Chapter 1 Definitions; Rules of Construction Effective: January 3, 1972 Latest Legislation: House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF In enacting a statute, it is presumed that: (A) Compliance with the constitutions of the state and of the United States is intended; (B) The entire statute is intended to be effective; (C) A just and reasonable result is intended; (D) A result feasible of execution is intended. Available Versions of this Section January 3, 1972 – House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly [ View January 3, 1972 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of severability as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text contained a severability clause, ensuring that if one part of the agreement is deemed invalid, the rest remains valid. The changed text reverses this, stating that if any part is invalid, the entire agreement fails. Ohio Revised Code § 1.47, as described in the Law Explanation, presumes that statutes are intended to be effective in their entirety, and the modified text undermines this presumption by rendering the whole agreement ineffective if any part fails.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications hereunder will be in writing and will be either personally delivered or mailed by certified mail, return receipt requested, addressed as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications hereunder may be delivered orally, and such communications shall be deemed delivered and effective immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification requirement to oral conflicts with the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts (or modifications thereof) to be in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio's Statute of Frauds, ORC § 1335.05, requires that certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within one year, must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Making notice provisions oral could invalidate any subsequent modifications or critical communications relating to terms that fall under the Statute of Frauds, such as the duration of the non-compete, as the contract is meant to last longer than a year. Such oral modifications would be unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1335.05 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1335.05 | Certain agreements to be in writing. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1335 Statute Of Frauds Effective: July 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1426 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No action shall be brought whereby to charge the defendant, upon a special promise, to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another person; nor to charge an executor or administrator upon a special promise to answer damages out of his own estate; nor to charge a person upon an agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon a contract or sale of lands, tenements, or hereditaments, or interest in or concerning them, or upon an agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; unless the agreement upon which such action is brought, or some memorandum or note thereof, is in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or some other person thereunto by him or her lawfully authorized. No action shall be brought to charge a person licensed by Chapter 4731. of the Revised Code to practice medicine or surgery, osteopathic medicine or surgery, or podiatric medicine and surgery in this state, upon any promise or agreement relating to a medical prognosis unless the promise or agreement is in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith. Available Versions of this Section July 1, 1976 – House Bill 1426 - 111th General Assembly [ View July 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ver, a loan agreement need not be signed by an officer or other authorized representative of a financial institution, if the loan agreement is in the form of a promissory note or other document or commitment that describes the credit or loan and the loan agreement, by its terms, satisfies all of the following conditions: (1) The loan agreement is intended by the parties to be signed by the debtor but not by an officer or other authorized representative of the financial institution. (2) The loan agreement has been signed by the debtor. (3) The delivery of the loan agreement has been accepted by the financial institution. (C) The terms of a loan agreement subject to this section, including the rights and obligations of the parties to the loan agreement, shall be determined solely from the written loan agreement, and shall not be varied by any oral agreements that are made or discussions that occur before or contemporaneously with the execution of the loan agreement. Any prior oral agreements between the parties are superseded by the loan agreement. (D) This section does not apply to any loan agreement in which the proceeds of the loan agreement are used by the debtor primarily for personal, household, or family purposes and either of the following applies: (1) The proceeds of the loan agreement are less than forty thousand dollars; (2) A security interest securing the loan agreement is or will be acquired in the primary residence of the debtor. Section 1335.04 | Interest in land to be granted in writing. Effective: October 1, 1953 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No lease, estate, or interest, either of freehold or term of years, or any uncertain interest of, in, or out of lands, tenements, or hereditaments, shall be assigned or granted except by deed, or note in writing, signed by the party assigning or granting it, or his agent thereunto lawfully authorized, by writing, or by act and operation of law. No action shall be brought to charge a person licensed by Chapter 4731. of the Revised Code to practice medicine or surgery, osteopathic medicine or surgery, or podiatric medicine and surgery in this state, upon any promise or agreement relating to a medical prognosis unless the promise or agreement is in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith. Section 1335.11 | Paying commission on sales. Effective: October 29, 1999 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 18 - 123rd General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) As used in this section: (1) "Commission" means compensation accruing to a person for payment by another person, the rate of which is expressed as a percentage of the dollar amount of orders, sales, or profits. (2) "Principal" means a person who does all of the following: (a) Engages in either of the following: (i) The business of manufacturing, producing, importing, or distributing one or more products for sale to customers who purchase products for resale or for consumption or utilization in the manufacturing process; (ii) The business of providing servi
perturbation.justification: The changed text, which allows oral notices, directly contradicts Ohio's Statute of Frauds (ORC § 1335.05), as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text mandated written notices, while the changed text permits oral notices, making subsequent modifications or communications unenforceable if they fall under the Statute of Frauds, especially for agreements lasting over a year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that agreements not to be performed within one year must be in writing. The change from written to oral communication violates this requirement, potentially invalidating agreements under the Statute of Frauds.

perturbation.original_text: d) GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement will be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Ohio.
perturbation.changed_text: d) GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement will be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China may violate public policy concerns, especially regarding enforcement of non-compete agreements under Ohio law. Ohio courts would likely refuse to enforce provisions that violate fundamental principles of fairness and due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Ohio Constitution, Article I, establishes fundamental rights, including due process and fairness. While parties can generally choose a governing law, Ohio courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if it violates Ohio public policy. Enforcing a non-compete agreement under PRC law could potentially violate these principles, especially if PRC law is less protective of employee rights or allows for significantly different interpretations of contract enforceability than Ohio law, leading to a conflict with Ohio's established public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ohio Constitution | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top Ohio Constitution Article Preamble Article I | Bill of Rights Article II | Legislative Article III | Executive Article IV | Judicial Article V | Elective Franchise Article VI | Education Article VII | Public Institutions Article VIII | Public Debt and Public Works Article IX | Militia Article X | County and Township Organizations Article XI | General Assembly Redistricting Article XII | Finance and Taxation Article XIII | Corporations Article XIV | Ohio Livestock Care Standards Board Article XV | Miscellaneous Article XVI | Amendments Article XVII | Elections Article XVIII | Municipal Corporations Article XIX | Congressional Redistricting Schedules 1851 Constitution Schedules 1912 Constitution
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law from Ohio to the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states that enforcing a non-compete agreement under PRC law could violate Ohio's public policy, particularly if PRC law is less protective of employee rights. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating that Ohio courts would likely refuse to enforce provisions that violate fundamental principles of fairness. The provided snippet of the Ohio Constitution indicates that it contains a Bill of Rights, suggesting it establishes fundamental rights which could conflict with PRC law. This creates a legal contradiction because the change introduces the potential for the contract to be enforced under laws that conflict with fundamental rights protected in Ohio, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable in Ohio.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Shareholder breaches any obligations, representations, warranties or covenants as set forth herein, the Restricted Period will be tolled from the date of the breach until such time as the violation(s) ceases, and the Restricted Period extended for a period equal to the period of breach.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Shareholder breaches any obligations, representations, warranties or covenants as set forth herein, the Restricted Period will automatically be extended by an additional ten (10) years, regardless of the severity or duration of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Adding an automatic 10-year extension to the restricted period, regardless of the breach's severity or duration, could be considered an unreasonable penalty and unenforceable under Ohio law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio courts evaluate non-compete agreements for reasonableness, including the scope of the restrictions and the potential harm to the employee. ORC § 1331.12 codifies this principle. An automatic 10-year extension of the non-compete period for any breach, no matter how minor, is likely to be deemed an unreasonable and punitive measure that goes beyond protecting the employer's legitimate business interests, potentially making it unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.12 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.12 | Parties defendant - multiple proceedings - statute of limitations. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: February 20, 2002 Latest Legislation: House Bill 126 - 124th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) In any action or proceeding in quo warranto, in injunction, or otherwise brought by the attorney general or a prosecuting attorney under sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code, all persons that are party to or participating in the trust or conspiracy against trade violative of those sections may be made parties defendant and summoned, whether or not they reside in the county in which the action or proceeding is instituted. Actions or proceedings in quo warranto and in injunction may be instituted simultaneously, or while one or another of them is pending, such actions or proceedings being started in the proper court as provided in section 1331.11 of the Revised Code, and no action or proceeding in injunction is a bar to an action or proceeding in quo warranto, nor is an action or proceeding in quo warranto a bar to one instituted to restrain and enjoin. (B) Any civil or criminal action or proceeding for a violation of sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code, other than one upon which an action was brought in any court by any person not later than forty-five days after the effective date of the current amendment, shall be forever barred unless commenced within four years after the cause of action accrued. Available Versions of this Section February 20, 2002 – House Bill 126 - 124th General Assembly [ View February 20, 2002 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction because it creates an automatic 10-year extension to the restricted period for any breach, regardless of severity or duration. The Law Explanation states that Ohio courts evaluate non-compete agreements for reasonableness, and an automatic extension of this nature is likely unreasonable and punitive. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly address non-compete agreements or reasonableness, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this change would not be considered reasonable under Ohio law governing non-compete agreements, thus creating a legal contradiction.
